title: Charmed Life - Chapter 4 (Fragment 2)
text:
Mrs. Sharp had been very kind. She was a witch too—the printed sign in her parlor window said Certified Witch —and interested in Gwendolen. The Mayor was a little dubious. Like all people who had no talent for witchcraft, he did not approve of those who had. He asked Cat how he felt about Gwendolen’s plan. Cat did not mind. He preferred living in the house he was used to, even if it was downstairs. Since the Mayor felt that the two orphans ought to be made as happy as possible, he agreed. Gwendolen and Cat moved in with Mrs. Sharp.

Looking back on it, Cat supposed that it was from this time on that he was certain Gwendolen was a witch. He had not been sure before. When he had asked his parents, they had shaken their heads, sighed, and looked unhappy. Cat had been puzzled, because he remembered the terrible trouble there had been when Gwendolen gave him cramps. He could not see how his parents could blame Gwendolen for it unless she truly was a witch. But all that was changed now. Mrs. Sharp made no secret of it.

“You’ve a real talent for magic, dearie,” she said, beaming at Gwendolen, “and I wouldn’t be doing my duty by you if I let it go to waste. We must see about a teacher for you right away. You could do worse than go to Mr. Nostrum next door for a start. He may be the worst necromancer in town, but he knows how to teach. He’ll give you a good grounding, my love.”

Mr. Nostrum’s charges for teaching magic turned out to be £1 an hour for the Elementary Grades, and a guinea an hour for the Advanced Grades beyond. Rather expensive, as Mrs. Sharp said. She put on her best hat with black beads and ran around to the Town Hall to see if the Fund would pay for Gwendolen’s lessons.

To her annoyance, the Mayor refused. He told Mrs. Sharp that witchcraft was not part of an ordinary education. Mrs. Sharp came back rattling the beads on her hat with irritation, and carrying a flat cardboard box the Mayor had given her, full of the odds and ends the kind ladies had cleared out of Gwendolen’s parents’ bedroom.

“Blind prejudice!” Mrs. Sharp said, dumping the box on the kitchen table. “If a person has a gift, they have a right to have it developed—and so I told him! But don’t worry, dearie,” she said, seeing that Gwendolen was looking decidedly stormy. “There’s a way around everything. Mr. Nostrum would teach you for nothing, if we found the right thing to tempt him with. Let’s have a look in this box. Your poor ma and pa may have left something that might be just the thing.”

Accordingly, Mrs. Sharp turned the box out onto the table. It was a queer collection of things—letters and lace and souvenirs. Cat did not remember having seen half of them before. There was a marriage certificate, saying that Francis John Chant had married Caroline Mary Chant twelve years ago at St. Margaret’s Church, Wolvercote, and a withered nosegay his mother must have carried at the wedding. Underneath that, he found some glittery earrings he had never seen his mother wear.

Mrs. Sharp’s hat rattled as she bent swiftly over these. “Those are diamond earrings!” she said. “Your ma must have had money! Now, if I took those to Mr. Nostrum—But we’d get more for them if I took them around to Mr. Larkins.” Mr. Larkins kept the junk shop on the corner of the street—except that it was not always exactly junk. Among the brass fenders and chipped crockery you could find quite valuable things, and also a discreet notice saying Exotic Supplies —which meant that Mr. Larkins also stocked bats’ wings, dried newts, and other ingredients of magic. There was no question that Mr. Larkins would be very interested in a pair of diamond earrings. Mrs. Sharp’s eyes pouched up, greedy and beady, as she put out her hand to pick up the earrings.

vocabulary.definition: The feeling or state of being annoyed.
vocabulary.example: To her annoyance, the Mayor refused.

vocabulary.definition: Smiling broadly; radiating happiness or joy.
vocabulary.example: “You’ve a real talent for magic, dearie,” she said, beaming at Gwendolen.

vocabulary.definition: Officially recognized or guaranteed.
vocabulary.example: She was a witch too—the printed sign in her parlor window said Certified Witch.

vocabulary.definition: The price demanded for a service or product.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Nostrum’s charges for teaching magic turned out to be £1 an hour for the Elementary Grades.

vocabulary.definition: A painful involuntary contraction of a muscle or muscles.
vocabulary.example: He remembered the terrible trouble there had been when Gwendolen gave him cramps.

vocabulary.definition: Careful and circumspect in one's speech or actions, especially in order to avoid causing offence.
vocabulary.example: Also a discreet notice saying Exotic Supplies.

vocabulary.definition: Hesitating or doubting; not to be relied upon; suspect.
vocabulary.example: The Mayor was a little dubious. Like all people who had no talent for witchcraft, he did not approve of those who had.

vocabulary.definition: A moral or legal obligation; a responsibility.
vocabulary.example: “I wouldn’t be doing my duty by you if I let it go to waste.”

vocabulary.definition: Originating in or characteristic of a distant foreign country; unusual and exciting.
vocabulary.example: Exotic Supplies —which meant that Mr. Larkins also stocked bats’ wings.

vocabulary.definition: A sum of money saved or made available for a particular purpose.
vocabulary.example: She ran around to the Town Hall to see if the Fund would pay.

vocabulary.definition: Having or showing an intense and selfish desire for something, especially wealth or power.
vocabulary.example: Mrs. Sharp’s eyes pouched up, greedy and beady.

vocabulary.definition: A solid basis of knowledge or training.
vocabulary.example: He’ll give you a good grounding, my love.

vocabulary.definition: The state of feeling annoyed, impatient, or slightly angry.
vocabulary.example: With irritation, and carrying a flat cardboard box...

vocabulary.definition: A person who practices necromancy, a form of magic involving communication with the dead.
vocabulary.example: He may be the worst necromancer in town, but he knows how to teach.

vocabulary.definition: Various small, miscellaneous items.
vocabulary.example: The Mayor had given her, full of the odds and ends the kind ladies had cleared out.

vocabulary.definition: A child whose parents are dead.
vocabulary.example: Since the Mayor felt that the two orphans ought to be made as happy as possible, he agreed.

vocabulary.definition: Preconceived opinion not based on reason or actual experience.
vocabulary.example: “Blind prejudice!” Mrs. Sharp said.

vocabulary.definition: Confused or perplexed.
vocabulary.example: Cat had been puzzled, because he remembered the terrible trouble there had been when Gwendolen gave him cramps.

vocabulary.definition: Made a rapid succession of short, sharp knocking sounds.
vocabulary.example: Mrs. Sharp’s hat rattled as she bent swiftly over these.

vocabulary.definition: Making a rapid succession of short, sharp knocking sounds.
vocabulary.example: Mrs. Sharp came back rattling the beads on her hat with irritation.

vocabulary.definition: To entice or attract someone to do something, especially something wrong or unwise.
vocabulary.example: If we found the right thing to tempt him with.

vocabulary.definition: Worth a great deal of money or having great importance.
vocabulary.example: You could find quite valuable things, and also a discreet notice.

vocabulary.definition: The practice of magical skills and abilities.
vocabulary.example: Like all people who had no talent for witchcraft, he did not approve of those who had.

vocabulary.definition: Shriveled or dried up, especially a plant or flower.
vocabulary.example: A withered nosegay his mother must have carried at the wedding.
